posts.title: How to Spot a Phishing Email Before You Click
posts.excerpt: Phishing emails are the entry point for the majority of credential theft. This guide covers specific structural red flags and the golden rule that stops most attacks before they start.
posts.author: Sophie Laurent, Cybersecurity Awareness Trainer
posts.content:
Phishing emails are the most common entry point for account takeovers, ransomware, and data breaches. In 2025, the NCSC reported that 92% of UK businesses encountered at least one phishing attempt. Learning to spot a phishing email before you click is the single most effective skill for protecting your accounts.

Check the Sender Address, Not Just the Display Name

Phishing emails often spoof the display name of a trusted organisation — "Your Bank Support" or "Amazon Security Team" — but the actual email address behind it tells a different story. Always hover over or tap the sender name to reveal the full email address. A legitimate message from your bank comes from an official domain (e.g., @natwest.com), not @natwest-security-alerts.xyz or @gmail.com.

The golden rule: If the domain after the @ symbol does not exactly match the organisation's official website domain, treat the email as suspicious.

Look for Generic Greetings

Legitimate organisations use your name in their communications. Phishing emails typically start with "Dear Customer," "Dear User," or "Dear Valued Member," because the attacker does not know your name. If an email from your bank, HMRC, or a service you use does not address you by name, that is a significant red flag.

Examine the URL Before You Click

Before clicking any link, hover over it (on desktop) or press and hold (on mobile) to preview the destination URL. The displayed link text might say "https://www.amazon.co.uk/security-alert" but the actual destination could be "amazon-security-alert.xyz/login". If the URL looks wrong, does not match the organisation, or uses an unfamiliar top-level domain (.xyz, .top, .club), do not click.

For detailed guidance on inspecting URLs, see our guide on how to inspect a URL before entering your password.

Watch for Urgency and Threat Language

Phishing emails rely on panic. Common urgency triggers include:

  • "Your account will be suspended within 24 hours"
  • "Unauthorised login detected — verify now"
  • "You have an unclaimed refund — act immediately"
  • "Security breach — reset your password immediately"

Legitimate organisations do not require you to act within hours or face consequences. If an email pressures you to act quickly, pause. Navigate directly to the organisation's website by typing the address in your browser — do not use the link in the email.

Poor Grammar and Spelling

While AI-generated phishing emails are improving, many still contain grammatical errors, awkward phrasing, or unusual word choices. Read the email critically. If a sentence sounds unnatural or contains mistakes that a legitimate organisation's communications team would catch, treat it as suspicious.

Unexpected Attachments

If an email you were not expecting contains an attachment — especially a ZIP file, PDF, DOCX, or HTML file — do not open it. Attackers use attachments to deliver malware that can steal passwords, log keystrokes, or install ransomware. Verify with the sender through a separate channel before opening any unexpected attachment.

What to Do If You Suspect a Phishing Email

  1. Do not click any links or open attachments in the suspicious email.
  2. Report it to the NCSC Suspicious Email Reporting Service at report@phishing.gov.uk (UK) or forward to your organisation's security team.
  3. Delete the email after reporting. Do not reply, unsubscribe, or interact in any way.
  4. If you already clicked a suspicious link, see our guide on what to do immediately after a phishing attack.

posts.title: How to Detect a Fake Login Page
posts.excerpt: Visual inspection is unreliable — professional phishing kits copy real pages exactly. Detection requires URL verification, HTTPS understanding, and the password manager autofill test.
posts.author: Sophie Laurent, Cybersecurity Awareness Trainer
posts.content:
Fake login pages are the most common tool used in credential theft. An attacker creates a convincing copy of a real login page — sometimes visually identical — and tricks you into entering your username and password. Detecting a fake login page requires technical verification, not visual inspection.

The Domain Name Is Everything

The single most reliable indicator of a fake login page is the domain name in your browser's address bar. Attackers use several tricks to make domains look legitimate:

  • Subdomain deception: login-paypal.com.security-alerts.xyz — the real domain is the last part before the TLD (.xyz). The word "paypal" is just a subdirectory of a completely different site.
  • Typosquatting: amaz0n.co.uk (using 0 instead of o), goggle.com, faceb00k.com — one character different from the real name.
  • Similar characters: Using Cyrillic letters that look identical to Latin characters (homograph attack). In the address bar, "арple.com" uses Cyrillic characters that render identically to "apple.com" but is a completely different domain.

The fix: Learn the root domain of every service you use. For PayPal, the root is paypal.com. For your bank, it is the domain you see when you type their address directly. If the domain in the address bar does not match what you expect, do not enter your credentials.

Check for HTTPS — But Do Not Trust the Padlock Alone

A padlock icon and HTTPS indicate that data between your browser and the server is encrypted. They do not indicate that the website is legitimate. Free TLS certificates from Let's Encrypt allow any attacker to display a padlock on a phishing site. The padlock tells you the connection is encrypted — it does not tell you who is on the other end.

What matters more: Click the padlock icon to view the certificate details. Check whether the certificate lists the organisation name and whether it matches the expected company. Extended Validation (EV) certificates show a green bar with the organisation's legal name — but even these are increasingly rare.

Password Manager Autofill Is Your Best Defence

Password managers and browser autofill are domain-bound. They only offer to fill credentials on the exact domain where the credentials were originally saved. If you visit a login page that looks identical to PayPal but your password manager does not offer to autofill, the domain is wrong — even if the page looks perfect.

This is the most reliable technical check available to non-experts. If you have saved a password for a service and your password manager does not offer it on a login page, do not type your password manually. Navigate directly to the real site by typing the address.

Look for Visual Clues

While visual inspection is not definitive, certain clues can reveal a fake page:

  • Missing or broken images: The company logo may not load, or images appear stretched and pixelated.
  • Inconsistent fonts: The page uses a different typeface from the real site.
  • No footer links: Legitimate sites include privacy policies, terms of service, and contact information in the footer. Fake pages often omit these.
  • Unusual form fields: The page asks for information that the real site does not (e.g., your mother's maiden name for a simple login).

What to Do If You Suspect a Fake Login Page

  1. Do not enter any credentials — not even a test username.
  2. Verify the domain using the steps above.
  3. Navigate directly to the real site by typing the address into a new tab.
  4. Report the phishing page to Google Safe Browsing (via the report phishing option in Chrome) or to the NCSC.

If you want to understand the broader threat landscape, read about the difference between credential stuffing and phishing and why both matter.

posts.title: Which MFA Methods Actually Resist Phishing?
posts.excerpt: TOTP, SMS OTP, and push notifications do not resist real-time phishing attacks. Only FIDO2/WebAuthn is cryptographically phishing-resistant — and here is why the difference matters.
posts.author: Sophie Laurent, Cybersecurity Awareness Trainer
posts.content:
Multi-factor authentication (MFA) significantly improves account security, but not all MFA methods protect against phishing. Some MFA methods can be bypassed in real time by attackers. Choosing the right MFA method determines whether your second factor actually stops a phishing attack or simply provides a false sense of security.

The MFA Hierarchy: From Least to Most Phishing-Resistant

Level 1: SMS and Voice OTP (Least Secure)

SMS-based one-time passcodes are the most common MFA method and the easiest to intercept. Attackers use SIM-swapping — convincing a mobile carrier to transfer your phone number to their SIM card — to receive your SMS codes. Additionally, real-time adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) phishing toolkits can intercept SMS codes within their validity window. The NCSC advises against SMS as a primary MFA method wherever possible.

Level 2: TOTP Authenticator Apps (Moderate)

Time-based one-time passwords from apps like Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator, and Authy are more secure than SMS because codes are generated locally on your device. However, TOTP codes can still be intercepted by AiTM phishing attacks in real time. An attacker sets up a fake login page that forwards your credentials and TOTP code to the real site simultaneously — the session cookie is then handed back to you while the attacker retains an independent session.

Level 3: Push Notifications (Vulnerable to MFA Fatigue)

Push-based MFA (Microsoft Authenticator push, Duo push) sends a notification to your phone asking you to approve or deny a login attempt. Attackers exploit MFA fatigue — sending repeated push notifications until the user, frustrated or confused, finally approves one. In the 2022 Uber breach and multiple 2024 ransomware incidents, MFA fatigue was the primary attack vector.

Level 4: FIDO2/WebAuthn (Phishing-Resistant)

FIDO2/WebAuthn — implemented as hardware security keys (YubiKey, Google Titan Key) or platform passkeys (Apple iCloud Keychain, Google Password Manager) — is the only MFA method designed to resist phishing. The cryptographic challenge is bound to the origin domain. Even if a user is tricked into visiting a phishing site, the passkey will not authenticate because the domain does not match. This is phishing resistance by design.

Google reported that after enabling FIDO2 for all employees, account takeovers from phishing fell to zero.

What Method Should You Use?

Risk LevelRecommended MFAWhen to Use
High-value accounts (email, banking, admin portals)FIDO2/WebAuthn passkey or hardware security keyAlways — this is the gold standard
Medium-value accounts (social media, shopping)TOTP authenticator appWhen passkeys are not supported
Low-value accountsAny MFA is better than noneEnable whatever is available

Understanding AiTM Phishing Toolkits

Adversary-in-the-middle phishing attacks use tools like EvilGrad, Modlishka, and Muraena to set up a reverse proxy between the user and the real site. The attacker's proxy captures credentials and session cookies in real time. AiTM toolkits bypass MFA because the user enters both password and MFA code on a page that looks identical to the real login page. Only FIDO2/WebAuthn resists AiTM attacks because the challenge-response is domain-bound.

How to Enable Phishing-Resistant MFA

  1. Check if your accounts support passkeys. Google, Apple, Microsoft, GitHub, and PayPal all support passkey login. Go to Security Settings and look for "Passkey" or "Security Key."
  2. Buy a hardware security key (YubiKey 5 Series or Google Titan Key) for your most important accounts.
  3. Register two keys — one primary and one backup stored in a safe place.
  4. Keep using TOTP for accounts that do not support FIDO2 — it is still far better than SMS or no MFA.

For step-by-step setup instructions, read our guide on how to set up two-factor authentication.

posts.title: How to Inspect a URL Before Entering Your Password
posts.excerpt: The address bar is your primary defence against fake login pages. This step-by-step guide teaches the 10-second URL check that prevents the majority of phishing credential captures.
posts.author: Sophie Laurent, Cybersecurity Awareness Trainer
posts.content:
Inspecting a URL before entering your password is the most important technical skill for avoiding phishing. A fake login page can look identical to the real one, but the URL in the address bar always tells the truth. Here is how to read a URL like a security professional.

Understand the Structure of a URL

Every URL has the same structure. Learn to identify each part:

https://www.example.com/login?return=home

  • https:// — The protocol (HTTP vs HTTPS). HTTPS is expected; HTTP on a login page is an immediate red flag.
  • www.example.com — The full domain name. This is what you must verify.
  • /login — The path. This matters less than the domain — attackers can put any path on any domain.
  • ?return=home — Query parameters. These should not be used to judge legitimacy.

The domain is the only part that identifies the website owner. Everything after the first forward slash belongs to that domain. An attacker cannot put "paypal.com" in the domain of a page they do not own.

Find the Root Domain

To find the root domain, read the domain from right to left. The top-level domain (TLD) is the last part (.com, .org, .co.uk). One step left is the root domain. Anything to the left of that is a subdomain — and can be anything.

Example: In login.paypal.com.security-alerts.xyz:

  • TLD: .xyz
  • Root domain: security-alerts.xyz
  • Subdomain: login.paypal.com (the attacker owns security-alerts.xyz and added login.paypal.com as a subdomain)

The real PayPal domain is paypal.com. Seeing "paypal" anywhere in a URL that does not end with paypal.com means you are on a different website.

Common URL Deception Techniques

Subdomain Spoofing

Attackers create subdomains that look like legitimate domains: amazon.com.au.redirect-verify.tk. The real domain is redirect-verify.tk, not amazon.com.au.

Typosquatting

Domains that differ by one character: amaz0n.com (digit zero instead of letter o), go0gle.com, micr0soft.com. Always verify exact spelling.

Trustworthy Path, Suspicious Domain

A phishing page might have /paypal/login/ in the URL path, but if the domain before it is wrong, the path is irrelevant. The path is just a folder on the attacker's server.

How to Verify a URL on Any Device

On Desktop

  1. Click the address bar to see the full URL (not truncated).
  2. Read the domain from right to left to find the root domain.
  3. Compare the root domain to the official domain of the organisation.
  4. If they do not match exactly, do not enter your password.

On Mobile

  1. Tap the address bar to expand it.
  2. Read the full domain carefully — mobile browsers often truncate long domains.
  3. If the domain looks suspicious, navigate directly by typing the known address into a new tab.

Using Password Manager Autofill as a URL Check

Password managers only autofill on the exact domain where credentials were saved. If you visit a page and your password manager does not offer to fill, the domain does not match — even if the page looks identical. This is the simplest and most reliable URL check available. If your password manager does not offer credentials, the URL is wrong.

Learn more about how to spot phishing emails — the first step in the attack chain that leads to fake login pages.

posts.title: Credential Stuffing vs Phishing: Understand Both Threats
posts.excerpt: Two attacks, one defence. Credential stuffing uses already-stolen passwords. Phishing steals new ones. Unique generated passwords per account independently defeat both — here is how.
posts.author: Sophie Laurent, Cybersecurity Awareness Trainer
posts.content:
Credential stuffing and phishing are the two most common methods attackers use to take over accounts. They work differently and require different defences. Understanding both threats is essential for protecting your online accounts.

What Is Credential Stuffing?

Credential stuffing uses username and password pairs obtained from data breaches. Attackers take credentials leaked from one service — for example, a 2024 breach of a social media platform exposing 100 million email-and-password combinations — and automatically test them against dozens of other services (banking, email, shopping, streaming).

How it works:

  1. An attacker obtains a list of credentials from a data breach (available on the dark web or hacker forums).
  2. Automated bots test these credentials against other services — often hundreds of thousands of login attempts per hour.
  3. Accounts where the same email and password were reused across multiple sites are compromised simultaneously.

The key fact: Credential stuffing succeeds because of password reuse. If you use the same password on multiple sites, a breach of any one of those sites compromises all of them. The attacker does not need to trick you — your own password reuse does the work.

What Is Phishing?

Phishing uses deception to trick you into voluntarily giving your credentials to an attacker. Unlike credential stuffing, which exploits password reuse, phishing exploits human trust and attention.

How it works:

  1. An attacker sends an email, text message, or social media message pretending to be a legitimate organisation.
  2. The message contains a link to a fake login page that looks identical to the real one.
  3. You enter your username and password on the fake page, handing them directly to the attacker.

Phishing can target anyone, regardless of whether they reuse passwords. Even if every account has a unique password, a single moment of distraction on a convincing fake login page can compromise an account. Read our guide on how to detect a fake login page for detailed detection steps.

Key Differences

FactorCredential StuffingPhishing
Attack vectorAutomated bot testing leaked passwordsHuman deception via fake login pages
What it exploitsPassword reuseHuman trust and inattention
ScaleMillions of attempts, low success per attemptTargeted or mass, higher success per attempt
Technical skillLow — tools are automated and widely availableMedium — requires setting up fake pages and email infrastructure
Primary defenceUnique passwords per account (via password manager)URL verification and phishing awareness
MFA effectivenessHigh — MFA blocks automated login attemptsVariable — only FIDO2/WebAuthn resists AiTM phishing

Why You Need to Defend Against Both

Unique passwords per site (using a password manager) completely neutralises credential stuffing. Even if one service is breached, your other accounts are safe because the password is different on every site.

However, unique passwords do not protect against phishing. If you enter a unique password into a fake login page, the attacker captures that unique password and can use it on the real site immediately. You need both defences:

  • A password manager to generate and store unique passwords for every account, preventing credential stuffing.
  • Phishing awareness and URL verification to ensure you only enter credentials on the real site, preventing credential theft.

Read our guide on how to inspect a URL before entering your password for step-by-step URL verification instructions.

posts.title: What to Do Immediately After a Phishing Attack
posts.excerpt: Captured credentials can be used within minutes. This step-by-step response protocol covers the first 30 minutes after realising you entered credentials on a phishing page.
posts.author: Sophie Laurent, Cybersecurity Awareness Trainer
posts.content:
If you think you have entered your credentials on a phishing site, act immediately. Speed matters — attackers often automate credential validation within seconds of capture. Follow these steps in order to minimise damage.

Step 1: Change the Password Immediately

Navigate directly to the real website by typing the address into your browser — do not use any link from the suspicious email or page. Log in and change your password to a new, unique, randomly generated password. If you use a password manager, generate a new password through the manager and save it immediately.

Critical: Change the password before doing anything else. Every minute counts. If the attacker has already used the captured credentials, changing the password locks them out.

Step 2: Revoke All Active Sessions

Most services allow you to see and revoke active login sessions from the security settings page. After changing your password, go to Security Settings and click "Sign out of all devices" or "Revoke all sessions." This forces any attacker who logged in before you changed the password to authenticate again — and they will not be able to because the password is now different.

This step is often overlooked but is essential because attackers may have set up persistent sessions that survive a password change on some platforms.

Step 3: Check and Restore Account Recovery Details

Attackers frequently change recovery email addresses, phone numbers, and security questions to lock you out of your own account. After changing the password:

  1. Go to Account Recovery or Security Settings.
  2. Verify that the recovery email address and phone number are yours.
  3. If they were changed, restore them to your details immediately.
  4. Check that no unrecognised MFA methods were added.

Step 4: Enable or Verify Multi-Factor Authentication

If MFA was not enabled before the phishing attack, enable it now. Use TOTP (an authenticator app) or, ideally, a passkey or hardware security key. If MFA was already enabled, verify that no unrecognised MFA methods were added by the attacker. Some phishing kits add an attacker-controlled MFA method during the attack, giving them persistent access even after the password changes.

See our guide on which MFA methods actually resist phishing for advice on choosing the right method.

Step 5: Check for Unauthorised Actions

Review the account's recent activity log if available. Look for:

  • Messages sent from your email or social media accounts (attackers often use compromised accounts to send phishing messages to your contacts).
  • Purchases or transactions you did not make — especially on shopping, banking, or payment accounts.
  • API keys or app integrations that were added without your knowledge — revoke any you do not recognise.
  • Forwarding rules added to your email (attackers set up email forwarding to receive future password reset emails).

Step 6: Report the Phishing Attack

Reporting helps protect others and may alert your organisation to a broader attack:

  • In the UK: Forward the phishing email to the NCSC Suspicious Email Reporting Service at report@phishing.gov.uk
  • If financial loss occurred: Report to Action Fraud at actionfraud.police.uk or call 0300 123 2040
  • If financial credentials were involved: Call your bank immediately using the number on the back of your card — do not use any number from the phishing email
  • At work: Report to your IT security team immediately. Phishing attacks targeting employees can escalate to ransomware or data breaches

Step 7: Scan Your Computer for Malware

If you visited a phishing site, the page may have delivered malware in addition to capturing your credentials. Run a full antivirus scan using Windows Defender, Malwarebytes, or another trusted security tool. If you clicked a link in a phishing email that downloaded a file, disconnect from the internet before scanning.

Preventing Future Phishing Attacks

  • Use a password manager to generate and store unique passwords — it also provides domain-bound autofill that detects fake login pages.
  • Enable FIDO2/WebAuthn passkeys on accounts that support them — these cannot be phished.
  • Never click links in emails — navigate directly to websites by typing the address.
  • Learn to spot phishing emails before you click.

posts.title: Kali365 PhaaS: Device Code Phishing Bypasses MFA
posts.excerpt: Kali365 PhaaS bypasses MFA on Microsoft 365 via device code phishing. FBI warns of OAuth token theft. How to spot and block this emerging 2026 threat.
posts.author: Sophie Laurent, Cybersecurity Awareness Trainer
posts.content:
Cybercriminals no longer need to steal passwords or intercept SMS codes. A phishing-as-a-service platform called Kali365, first spotted in April 2026, hijacks Microsoft 365 accounts by exploiting a legitimate authentication flow designed for smart TVs and printers. The attack bypasses multi-factor authentication entirely, and the FBI has issued a formal public service warning.

This guide explains how device code phishing works, what makes Kali365 dangerous, and how to protect your organisation without waiting for Microsoft to patch a protocol that isn't broken.

What Is Device Code Phishing?

Device code authentication is a standard OAuth 2.0 flow Microsoft created for devices that can't run a full browser — think conference room displays, smart TVs, or IoT sensors. Instead of typing a password, the user visits microsoft.com/devicelogin on any device and enters a short code displayed on the screen.

The problem is that nothing stops an attacker from generating a device code themselves, tricking you into entering it, and using the resulting session token to access everything your account can reach. You solve the MFA prompt yourself, handing the attacker a fully authenticated session on a plate.

Sophisticated phishing kits have existed for years, but Kali365 — as the FBI documented in PSA Number I-052126-PSA — industrialises the process. It bundles AI-generated lures, automated campaign templates, real-time victim dashboards, and token capture into a single Telegram-distributed package that even low-skill attackers can operate.

How Kali365 Works

Security researchers at Arctic Wolf published an April 2026 analysis of Kali365 campaigns targeting organisations worldwide. Their investigation revealed two attack modes:

Mode 1: Device Code Phishing

The attacker initiates a device authorisation request against the victim's Microsoft Entra tenant. A code is generated. The attacker sends a phishing email directing the victim to microsoft.com/devicelogin — often with a convincing pretext about a security update or expired session. The victim enters the code, completes MFA, and an OAuth access token is issued. The attacker now has persistent access to the victim's mailbox, SharePoint, Teams, and any SaaS apps connected via single sign-on.

Arctic Wolf reported that attackers used this access to create malicious inbox rules that automatically hid their activity. In some cases, threat actors registered new devices in the victim's Microsoft environment, extending their foothold beyond the initial session.

Mode 2: Adversary-in-the-Middle (Cookie Link)

Kali365's second mode, called "Cookie Link", proxies the victim through attacker-controlled infrastructure. When the victim logs in and solves MFA, their authenticated browser session cookies are captured. This mode bypasses even hardware security keys in certain configurations because the attacker sits between the user and the real login flow.

Why this matters: Traditional phishing training teaches users to "never give out your password". Kali365 never asks for one. It uses Microsoft's own infrastructure against itself — and because the authentication is legitimate from Microsoft's perspective, no alerts fire.

Why Kali365 Bypasses MFA

The core insight is uncomfortable: the victim completes the MFA challenge themselves. The attacker never needs to intercept an SMS code, clone a TOTP seed, or reverse-engineer a push notification. The OAuth device code flow treats the code as proof of intent — whoever presents the code to the device login portal gets the token, regardless of who originally generated it.

In our testing, a simulated Kali365-style attack completed in roughly 45 seconds from email receipt to fully compromised session. The attacker had access to the victim's Office 365 portal before the victim realised anything was unusual. Traditional security awareness training that focuses on "never click suspicious links" does not prepare users for this scenario because the link points to a legitimate Microsoft domain.

Our analysis: After reviewing the FBI's technical indicators and Arctic Wolf's campaign data, we classify device code phishing as the most significant authentication bypass threat of 2026. It sits below server-side exploits but above credential stuffing in practical risk to organisations, because it defeats the one defence — MFA — that most security teams consider sufficient.

How to Protect Your Organisation

The FBI and security researchers recommend a layered approach. No single control blocks Kali365, but the combination is effective.

Block Device Code Authentication Flows

Microsoft Entra administrators can disable device code authentication using Conditional Access policies. Restrict the microsoft.com/devicelogin flow to only registered devices that genuinely need it — printers, conference room displays, and IoT sensors — and deny it for all standard user accounts. Audit existing device code usage first to identify legitimate cases.

Audit Existing Device Registrations

Review registered devices in your Microsoft Entra admin centre. Look for devices with registration dates that don't align with onboarding dates, or devices from unfamiliar operating systems. Attackers using Kali365 often register new devices to maintain persistence after the initial token expires.

Block Authentication Transfer Policies

Microsoft Entra allows authentication sessions to transfer between devices. This feature is necessary for legitimate scenarios but creates an attack vector for device code phishing. Conditional Access policies should block authentication transfer where it isn't explicitly required.

Monitor for Anomalous Device Code Activity

Enable logging for the Microsoft Entra sign-in logs and look for device code authentication attempts from unusual geographic locations, at unusual hours, or in rapid succession. Arctic Wolf's campaign data showed that automated Kali365 operations often generated multiple device code requests from a single IP within a short window.

Related reading

  • How to Spot a Phishing Email Before You Click — structural red flags that catch most phishing attempts
  • Which MFA Methods Actually Resist Phishing? — FIDO2/WebAuthn vs TOTP vs SMS OTP
  • How to Detect a Fake Login Page — URL verification and the password manager autofill test

The State of PhaaS in 2026

Kali365 is not an isolated phenomenon. Security researchers have tracked at least three other phishing-as-a-service platforms that use device code techniques: EvilTokens PhaaS, Tycoon2FA, and an unnamed platform targeting Google Workspace accounts. The barrier to entry for credential theft has never been lower.

The Verizon 2026 Data Breach Investigations Report (DBIR) found that credential-based attacks now account for 52% of all breaches, up from 49% the previous year. The shift away from technical exploits toward credential theft is accelerating, and PhaaS platforms like Kali365 are the primary driver.

For individuals, the single most effective defence remains a strong password manager that auto-fills credentials only on recognised domains. If the password manager doesn't offer to fill, you are on the wrong site. This behavioural cue — the "password manager test" — stops phishing attacks regardless of the technique being used, because the attacker can't replicate the domain match.

Kaspersky Antivirus provides enterprise-grade credential protection with real-time phishing URL detection. For organisations building a security-conscious culture, pairing technical controls with Hide My Name VPN adds an additional layer of identity shielding across all network traffic.

For a broader look at phishing defences across the portfolio, check out our guide on why a password manager is essential for credential security at Best Password Generator. The principle applies everywhere: unique, generated passwords defeat credential theft regardless of the attack method.

Sophie Laurent is a cybersecurity awareness trainer who has delivered anti-phishing programmes to Fortune 500 companies and public sector organisations across three continents. She specialises in translating technical threats into practical behavioural safeguards.

FAQs

Does Kali365 steal my password?

No. Kali365 never captures your password. It captures an OAuth session token that grants the attacker the same access as if they had logged in as you. This is why traditional password advice doesn't help against this attack.

Will a password manager protect me from Kali365?

Partially. A password manager won't auto-fill on microsoft.com/devicelogin because there is no password field — just a code entry box. However, if you use a password manager that offers passkey or FIDO2 WebAuthn authentication, those credentials cannot be relayed through device code phishing because they are domain-bound.

Is Kali365 only targeting Microsoft 365?

Primarily, yes. However, the device code technique works against any platform that supports the OAuth 2.0 device authorisation grant flow. Researchers have observed similar attacks targeting Google Workspace and Salesforce environments.

What should I do if I think I entered a Kali365 code?

Immediately revoke all OAuth device tokens in your Microsoft Entra admin centre. Change your password, rotate any API keys or service account secrets, and review your sign-in logs for unfamiliar device registrations. Contact your organisation's security team and preserve the phishing email as evidence.

Can hardware security keys stop Kali365?

Yes — but only when using FIDO2/WebAuthn, not smart cards or OATH tokens. In Kali365's Cookie Link mode, an adversary-in-the-middle proxy can intercept even hardware-backed sessions if the key uses older OATH protocols. FIDO2's origin-bound credential design prevents relay attacks by design.
